name: Opening Of Mouth
shortDescription: The Opening of the Mouth was a crucial ancient Egyptian ritual performed on mummies and statues to restore their ability to breathe, speak, eat, and move in the afterlife.
description: The Opening of the Mouth (wp-rt) was a complex and vital funerary ritual in ancient Egypt, intended to magically restore the senses and faculties of the deceased or a statue, allowing them to live and function in the afterlife. The ceremony involved a series of symbolic acts and recitations, performed by priests, often wearing masks of deities like Anubis. Tools such as a 'peseshkef' knife, a double-bladed adze, and other instruments were used to symbolically 'open' the mouth, eyes, ears, and other body parts. This act was believed to reanimate the mummy, enabling the deceased to breathe, speak, eat, and move freely in the Duat (the underworld) and to partake in offerings. The ritual was not only performed on mummies but also on statues of gods and pharaohs to imbue them with divine power and ensure their continued efficacy. The ceremony involved specific incantations and spells from texts like the Book of the Dead, designed to reactivate the vital functions of the deceased and ensure their successful transition into the afterlife. The Opening of the Mouth was considered essential for the deceased to be fully resurrected and participate in the eternal life promised by Egyptian religious beliefs. Canopic jars, used to store the organs of the deceased, were also important to preserve the body for this crucial ritual.

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead: The Book of Going Forth by Day
sources.author: Translation by Raymond O. Faulkner

sources.title: Religion and Magic in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Rosalie David

sources.title: Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Jan Assmann
